preamble.summary: Isaiah 57 opens with a lament that the righteous perish and no one takes it to heart — their death is actually a mercy, sparing them from the evil to come. The chapter then unleashes a blistering indictment of idolatry, exposing Israel's spiritual adultery in valleys, under oaks, and on high mountains. Yet the chapter pivots dramatically at verse 15 to one of the most profound theology-of-presence statements in all of Scripture: the High and Exalted One dwells with the crushed and lowly in spirit. The chapter closes with the offer of peace — but not for the wicked.
preamble.remarkable: Verse 15 is among the most theologically dense verses in Isaiah. It holds two seemingly irreconcilable truths in tension: God inhabits eternity and the high holy place, yet He simultaneously dwells with the contrite and lowly. This is not condescension but divine preference. Verse 19 anticipates Ephesians 2:17 ('peace to those who were far off and peace to those who were near'). Verse 21's refrain — 'There is no peace for the wicked' — is the second of three occurrences that structurally divide Isaiah 40–66 into three sections of nine chapters each.
preamble.friction: The idolatry passages (vv.5-10) contain sexually charged language describing Israel's unfaithfulness. We have rendered these with fidelity to the prophetic rhetoric without sanitizing the force of the metaphor, as the shock is intentional to the prophetic message.
preamble.connections: Verse 15's theology of divine presence with the lowly connects to Psalm 34:18, Psalm 51:17, and Jesus' Beatitudes (Matt 5:3-4). Verse 19's 'peace to the far and near' is quoted in Ephesians 2:17. The 'no peace for the wicked' refrain (v.21) echoes 48:22 and anticipates 66:24.

verses.text_hebrew: הַצַּדִּיק אָבָד וְאֵין אִישׁ שָׂם עַל־לֵב וְאַנְשֵׁי־חֶסֶד נֶאֱסָפִים בְּאֵין מֵבִין כִּי־מִפְּנֵי הָרָעָה נֶאֱסַף הַצַּדִּיק
verses.text_kjv: The righteous perisheth, and no man layeth it to heart: and merciful men are taken away, none considering that the righteous is taken away from the evil to come.
verses.rendering:
The righteous one perishes,
and no one takes it to heart.
Men of steadfast love are gathered away,
with no one understanding
that the righteous is taken away
from the evil to come.
verses.expanded_rendering: The anshei chesed (men of steadfast love, covenant-loyal ones) are swept away in death — yet this is a hidden mercy, for they are spared the coming calamity.
translator_notes: 'Men of steadfast love' (anshei chesed) — those defined by covenant faithfulness. Their death is reframed as divine protection, not divine failure.

verses.text_hebrew: יָבוֹא שָׁלוֹם יָנוּחוּ עַל־מִשְׁכְּבוֹתָם הֹלֵךְ נְכֹחוֹ
verses.text_kjv: He shall enter into peace: they shall rest in their beds, each one walking in his uprightness.
translator_notes: Death for the righteous is described as entering shalom — wholeness and rest. This is one of the Old Testament's gentlest depictions of death.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאַתֶּם קִרְבוּ הֵנָּה בְּנֵי עֹנְנָה זֶרַע מְנָאֵף וַתִּזְנֶה
verses.text_kjv: But draw near hither, ye sons of the sorceress, the seed of the adulterer and the whore.
verses.rendering:
But you — draw near, you sons of a sorceress,
you offspring of the adulterer and the prostitute!
translator_notes: The tone shifts violently. The tender eulogy for the righteous gives way to a courtroom summons against the idolaters.

verses.text_hebrew: עַל־מִי תִּתְעַנָּגוּ עַל־מִי תַּרְחִיבוּ פֶה תַּאֲרִיכוּ לָשׁוֹן הֲלוֹא־אַתֶּם יִלְדֵי־פֶשַׁע זֶרַע שָׁקֶר
verses.text_kjv: Against whom do ye sport yourselves? against whom make ye a wide mouth, and draw out the tongue? are ye not children of transgression, a seed of falsehood,
verses.rendering:
Whom are you mocking?
Against whom do you open wide your mouth
and stick out your tongue?
Are you not children of transgression,
offspring of deceit?
translator_notes: The mocking gestures described (wide mouth, extended tongue) indicate contempt — likely directed at the prophets or at YHWH Himself.

verses.text_hebrew: הַנֵּחָמִים בָּאֵלִים תַּחַת כָּל־עֵץ רַעֲנָן שֹׁחֲטֵי הַיְלָדִים בַּנְּחָלִים תַּחַת סְעִפֵי הַסְּלָעִים
verses.text_kjv: Enflaming yourselves with idols under every green tree, slaying the children in the valleys under the clifts of the rocks?
verses.rendering:
You who burn with lust among the oaks,
under every green tree —
who slaughter children in the valleys,
under the clefts of the rocks!
translator_notes: 'Slaughter children' refers to child sacrifice, likely to Molech — the most horrifying expression of Israel's apostasy. The valley likely refers to the Valley of Hinnom (Gehenna).

verses.text_hebrew: בְּחַלְּקֵי־נַחַל חֶלְקֵךְ הֵם הֵם גּוֹרָלֵךְ גַּם־לָהֶם שָׁפַכְתְּ נֶסֶךְ הֶעֱלִית מִנְחָה הַעַל־אֵלֶּה אֶנָּחֵם
verses.text_kjv: Among the smooth stones of the stream is thy portion; they, they are thy lot: even to them hast thou poured a drink offering, thou hast offered a meat offering. Should I receive comfort in these?
translator_notes: A bitter wordplay: chalqei nachal (smooth stones of the valley) sounds like cheleq (portion/inheritance). Israel's 'inheritance' has become river rocks used in pagan worship.

verses.text_hebrew: עַל הַר־גָּבֹהַּ וְנִשָּׂא שַׂמְתְּ מִשְׁכָּבֵךְ גַּם־שָׁם עָלִית לִזְבֹּחַ זָבַח
verses.text_kjv: Upon a lofty and high mountain hast thou set thy bed: even thither wentest thou up to offer sacrifice.
translator_notes: 'High places' were hilltop shrines associated with Canaanite worship. The 'bed' imagery merges sexual and religious infidelity.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאַחַר הַדֶּלֶת וְהַמְּזוּזָה שַׂמְתְּ זִכְרוֹנֵךְ כִּי מֵאִתִּי גִּלִּית וַתַּעֲלִי הִרְחַבְתְּ מִשְׁכָּבֵךְ וַתִּכְרָת־לָךְ מֵהֶם אָהַבְתְּ מִשְׁכָּבָם יָד חָזִית
verses.text_kjv: Behind the doors also and the posts hast thou set up thy remembrance: for thou hast discovered thyself to another than me, and art gone up; thou hast enlarged thy bed, and made thee a covenant with them; thou lovedst their bed where thou sawest it.
verses.rendering:
Behind the door and the doorpost
you have set up your pagan symbols.
Deserted Me and uncovered yourself —
you climbed up and made your bed wide.
You made a pact with them;
you loved their bed;
you gazed on their nakedness.
translator_notes: Where the mezuzah should display God's words (Deut 6:9), they have placed pagan memorial symbols — a direct inversion of covenant faithfulness.

verses.text_hebrew: וַתָּשֻׁרִי לַמֶּלֶךְ בַּשֶּׁמֶן וַתַּרְבִּי רִקֻּחָיִךְ וַתְּשַׁלְּחִי צִרַיִךְ עַד־מֵרָחֹק וַתַּשְׁפִּילִי עַד־שְׁאוֹל
verses.text_kjv: And thou wentest to the king with ointment, and didst increase thy perfumes, and didst send thy messengers far off, and didst debase thyself even unto hell.
verses.rendering:
You journeyed to the king with oil
and multiplied your perfumes.
You sent your envoys far away
and debased yourself even to Sheol.
translator_notes: 'The king' (melekh) may refer to the pagan deity Molech (same consonants in Hebrew) or to foreign political alliances — both are acts of unfaithfulness to YHWH.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּרֹב דַּרְכֵּךְ יָגַעַתְּ לֹא אָמַרְתְּ נוֹאָשׁ חַיַּת יָדֵךְ מָצָאת עַל־כֵּן לֹא חָלִית
verses.text_kjv: Thou art wearied in the greatness of thy way; yet saidst thou not, There is no hope: thou hast found the life of thine hand; therefore thou wast not grieved.
verses.rendering:
You wearied yourself on your many journeys,
but you never said, 'It is hopeless.'
You found renewal of strength
and so did not grow faint.
translator_notes: The tragic irony: Israel's idolatrous energy never flags. She finds just enough false vitality in her adultery to keep pursuing it.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאֶת־מִי דָּאַגְתְּ וַתִּירְאִי כִּי תְכַזֵּבִי וְאוֹתִי לֹא זָכַרְתְּ לֹא־שַׂמְתְּ עַל־לִבֵּךְ הֲלֹא אֲנִי מַחְשֶׁה וּמֵעֹלָם וְאוֹתִי לֹא תִירָאִי
verses.text_kjv: And of whom hast thou been afraid or feared, that thou hast lied, and hast not remembered me, nor laid it to thy heart? have not I held my peace even of old, and thou fearest me not?
verses.rendering:
Whom did you dread and fear,
that you lied and did not remember Me
or take it to heart?
Have I not held My peace for a long time,
and so you do not fear Me?
translator_notes: God's long silence (machsheh) is revealed as the very thing that enabled Israel's recklessness — they mistook patience for indifference.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲנִי אַגִּיד צִדְקָתֵךְ וְאֶת־מַעֲשַׂיִךְ וְלֹא יוֹעִילוּךְ
verses.text_kjv: I will declare thy righteousness, and thy works; for they shall not profit thee.
verses.rendering:
I will expose your so-called righteousness
and your works — they will not profit you.
translator_notes: 'Your righteousness' is spoken with biting irony — Israel's self-assessed righteousness will be publicly deconstructed.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּזַעֲקֵךְ יַצִּילֻךְ קִבּוּצַיִךְ וְאֶת־כֻּלָּם יִשָּׂא־רוּחַ יִקַּח־הָבֶל וְהַחוֹסֶה בִי יִנְחַל־אֶרֶץ וְיִירַשׁ הַר־קָדְשִׁי
verses.text_kjv: When thou criest, let thy companies deliver thee; but the wind shall carry them all away; vanity shall take them: but he that putteth his trust in me shall possess the land, and shall inherit my holy mountain;
verses.rendering:
When you cry out, let your collection of idols deliver you!
But the wind will carry them all away;
a breath will take them.
But the one who takes refuge in Me
shall inherit the land
and possess My holy mountain.
translator_notes: The pivot: idols are carried off by wind (ruach), but those who trust YHWH inherit the land. The same ruach that destroys the false gives life to the faithful.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאָמַר סֹלּוּ־סֹלּוּ פַּנּוּ־דָרֶךְ הָרִימוּ מִכְשׁוֹל מִדֶּרֶךְ עַמִּי
verses.text_kjv: And shall say, Cast ye up, cast ye up, prepare the way, take up the stumblingblock out of the way of my people.
verses.rendering:
And it shall be said:
'Build up, build up! Prepare the way!
Remove every obstacle from the path of My people.'
translator_notes: This echoes the highway language of Isaiah 40:3 — God is again preparing a way for His people's return.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּי כֹה אָמַר רָם וְנִשָּׂא שֹׁכֵן עַד וְקָדוֹשׁ שְׁמוֹ מָרוֹם וְקָדוֹשׁ אֶשְׁכּוֹן וְאֶת־דַּכָּא וּשְׁפַל־רוּחַ לְהַחֲיוֹת רוּחַ שְׁפָלִים וּלְהַחֲיוֹת לֵב נִדְכָּאִים
verses.text_kjv: For thus saith the high and lofty One that inhabiteth eternity, whose name is Holy; I dwell in the high and holy place, with him also that is of a contrite and humble spirit, to revive the spirit of the humble, and to revive the heart of the contrite ones.
verses.rendering:
For this is what the High and Exalted One says —
He who inhabits eternity, whose name is Holy:
'I dwell in the high and holy place,
and also with the one who is crushed and lowly in spirit —
to revive the spirit of the lowly
and to revive the heart of the crushed.'

key_terms.semantic_range: crushed, contrite, broken, ground to dust
key_terms.note: God dwells with the dakka ('crushed one') — the same root as Psalm 34:18 ('the LORD is near to the brokenhearted') and Isaiah 53:5 ('crushed for our iniquities'). The Most High inhabits both eternity and the lowest human experience simultaneously.

key_terms.semantic_range: humble in spirit, low in spirit, those of a low disposition
key_terms.note: The phrase shefal ruach ('lowly/humble in spirit') pairs with dakka to describe those God chooses to dwell with — not the exalted but the broken. The purpose is restoration: 'to revive the spirit of the lowly, to revive the heart of the crushed.'

translator_notes: This verse is one of the theological peaks of Isaiah. The God who is ram venissa (high and exalted) — the same phrase from Isaiah 6:1 — chooses to dwell with the dakka (crushed). The paradox is the point.
'Inhabits eternity' (shokhen ad) — God's dwelling is not merely a place but a mode of being. He is both transcendent and intimately present.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּי לֹא לְעוֹלָם אָרִיב וְלֹא לָנֶצַח אֶקְצוֹף כִּי־רוּחַ מִלְּפָנַי יַעֲטוֹף וּנְשָׁמוֹת אֲנִי עָשִׂיתִי
verses.text_kjv: For I will not contend for ever, neither will I be always wroth: for the spirit should fail before me, and the souls which I have made.
verses.rendering:
For I will not contend forever,
nor will I always be angry,
for the spirit would grow faint before Me —
the very breath of life that I have made.
translator_notes: God self-limits His wrath because the creatures He made could not survive it indefinitely. His anger is real but bounded by His creative compassion.

verses.text_hebrew: בַּעֲוֹן בִּצְעוֹ קָצַפְתִּי וְאַכֵּהוּ הַסְתֵּר וְאֶקְצֹף וַיֵּלֶךְ שׁוֹבָב בְּדֶרֶךְ לִבּוֹ
verses.text_kjv: For the iniquity of his covetousness was I wroth, and smote him: I hid me, and was wroth, and he went on frowardly in the way of his heart.
verses.rendering:
Because of his wicked greed I was angry;
I struck him and hid My face in wrath.
Yet he kept turning away,
following the path of his own heart.
translator_notes: Even divine discipline did not produce repentance — Israel 'kept turning away' (shobab), a stubborn backsliding despite the blows.

verses.text_hebrew: דְּרָכָיו רָאִיתִי וְאֶרְפָּאֵהוּ וְאַנְחֵהוּ וַאֲשַׁלֵּם נִחֻמִים לוֹ וְלַאֲבֵלָיו
verses.text_kjv: I have seen his ways, and will heal him: I will lead him also, and restore comforts unto him and to his mourners.
verses.rendering:
I have seen his ways, but I will heal him.
I will lead him and restore comfort
to him and to his mourners.
translator_notes: The 'but' is implicit and crucial: God sees the rebellion and chooses healing anyway. Grace is not ignorance of sin but sovereignty over it.

verses.text_hebrew: בּוֹרֵא נִיב שְׂפָתָיִם שָׁלוֹם שָׁלוֹם לָרָחוֹק וְלַקָּרוֹב אָמַר יְהוָה וּרְפָאתִיו
verses.text_kjv: I create the fruit of the lips; Peace, peace to him that is far off, and to him that is near, saith the LORD; and I will heal him.
verses.rendering:
creating the fruit of the lips:
'Peace, peace to the one who is far off
and to the one who is near,'
says the LORD, 'and I will heal him.'

key_terms.semantic_range: peace peace, wholeness wholeness, complete well-being
key_terms.note: The doubled shalom shalom ('peace, peace') echoes 26:3 and extends healing to both 'the far and the near' — those in exile and those who remained. The doubling intensifies: this is not partial peace but total restoration.

translator_notes: Paul quotes this verse in Ephesians 2:17, identifying 'those far off' as Gentiles and 'those near' as Jews — both reconciled through Christ.
'Creating the fruit of the lips' — God creates the very praise that responds to His healing. The thanksgiving itself is a divine gift.

verses.text_hebrew: וְהָרְשָׁעִים כַּיָּם נִגְרָשׁ כִּי הַשְׁקֵט לֹא יוּכָל וַיִּגְרְשׁוּ מֵימָיו רֶפֶשׁ וָטִיט
verses.text_kjv: But the wicked are like the troubled sea, when it cannot rest, whose waters cast up mire and dirt.
translator_notes: The sea image captures restless, self-destructive agitation — the wicked cannot find the shalom just offered because their own nature prevents it.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵין שָׁלוֹם אָמַר אֱלֹהַי לָרְשָׁעִים
verses.text_kjv: There is no peace, saith my God, unto the wicked.
verses.rendering:
'There is no peace,' says my God,
'for the wicked.'
translator_notes: This refrain (also at 48:22) divides Isaiah's final 27 chapters into three sections of nine chapters each (40-48, 49-57, 58-66). It is a structural and theological marker: God's peace is real but not universal — it excludes those who refuse it.
